title: Emery Castillo — Head of Marketing, Anchor Codec
compiled_truth:
Emery Castillo joined [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) in October 2026 as Head of Marketing, brought on to build the go-to-market engine from scratch. Before Anchor, Emery had spent the better part of a decade leading marketing at developer-tools and API-focused companies, guiding two separate startups through Series A and B growth phases. That background gave them a particular fluency in technical audiences — the kind of buyers who ignore traditional demand gen and respond almost exclusively to substance, docs, and word-of-mouth.

Castillo's approach is distribution-focused to the core. They believe — and have said this repeatedly in internal all-hands — that for infrastructure companies, "the product is the content." Rather than building a brand campaign first, Emery prioritized launching a technical blog, a changelog with personality, and a set of developer-facing case studies within the first sixty days. The early blog posts were raw, sometimes a little rough around the edges, but they resonated. One post on deterministic data primitives and why they matter for audit trails got picked up widely in infra-adjacent communities and drove a noticeable spike in inbound demo requests.

As a storyteller, Emery has a knack for translating dense infrastructure concepts into narratives that land. They've been instrumental in shaping how [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) talks about single-pass primitives externally — moving the messaging away from pure technical jargon toward problem-oriented framing. Internally, some engineers were initially skeptical of this shift, but early pipeline numbers quieted most objections.

Emery runs a lean team — just two direct reports as of mid-2027 — and is known for being scrappy and opinionated about channel selection. They avoid paid acquisition almost entirely, favoring organic community building, conference appearances, and co-marketing with early design partners. Castillo has a habit of testing messaging in Slack communities and on social before committing anything to the website, which has led to some surprisingly effective positioning pivots.

One quirk: Emery keeps a running document they call the "narrative debt log," tracking every place where Anchor's external messaging lags behind what the product actually does. It's become something of a living artifact that the whole team references during planning cycles. They're known for being direct in feedback, occassionally blunt, but universally respected for the rigor they bring to a function that infrastructure startups often underinvest in.
timeline:
- **2026-10-01** | Emery Castillo joins [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) as Head of Marketing, first non-engineering leadership hire.
- **2026-11-14** | Launches the Anchor technical blog and public changelog; first post covers the case for deterministic pipelines.
- **2026-12-09** | Publishes a developer case study co-authored with an early design partner, driving first major spike in organic inbound.
- **2027-02-22** | Presents "Marketing Infrastructure to Infrastructure Buyers" at a developer-tools community meetup (virtual).
- **2027-04-10** | Hires first marketing team member, a developer advocate with background in API documentation.
- **2027-06-18** | Introduces the "narrative debt log" as a formal planning artifact shared across engineering and GTM teams.
- **2027-09-05** | Leads messaging overhaul ahead of Anchor's Series A announcement, shifting external positioning toward problem-oriented framing.
- **2027-11-30** | Second marketing hire starts — a content marketer focused on long-form technical writing and SEO.
- **2028-01-22** | Emery keynotes at a small infrastructure-focused conference, talk titled "Why Your Docs Are Your Best Sales Rep."
- **2028-04-08** | Oversees launch of Anchor's first integrated product campaign tying together compression, authentication, and transmission messaging under a unified narrative.

_facts.name: Emery Castillo
_facts.title: Head of Marketing
_facts.background: Led marketing for developer-tools and API companies through Series A and B growth phases
